questions.question:
In a certain operating system, deadlock prevention is attempted using the following scheme. Each process is assigned a unique timestamp, and is restarted with the same timestamp if killed. Let P_i be the process holding a resource R, P_j be a process requesting for the same resource R, and T(P_i), T(P_j) be their timestamps respectively. The decision to wait or preempt one of the processes is based on the following algorithm:
If T(P_i) < T(P_j) then
    wait
else
    abort and restart P_j
end if

Which one of the following is TRUE?
A. The scheme is deadlock-free, but not starvation-free
B. The scheme is not deadlock-free, but starvation-free
C. The scheme is neither deadlock-free nor starvation-free
D. The scheme is both deadlock-free and starvation-free
questions.answer: D. The scheme is both deadlock-free and starvation-free
questions.to_find: Determine whether the given timestamp-based deadlock prevention scheme is deadlock-free and/or starvation-free.

understand.plain: Each process gets a unique timestamp when created. If process P_j requests a resource held by P_i, P_j only waits if P_i is older (smaller timestamp). If P_j is older or equal, P_j is aborted and restarted with the same old timestamp. Since a killed process keeps its original timestamp it eventually becomes the oldest and will always get to wait, preventing starvation.

keywords.explain: Assigns each process a unique timestamp at creation; timestamps are used to decide whether a process waits or is preempted when resource conflict occurs.
keywords.example: If P1 (timestamp 5) holds R and P2 (timestamp 10) requests R: T(P1)<T(P2) so P2 waits. If P3 (timestamp 3) requests R: T(P1)>T(P3) so P3 is aborted.

keywords.explain: An older process (smaller timestamp) requesting a resource held by a younger process waits; a younger process requesting a resource held by an older process is killed (dies) and restarted.
keywords.example: P_old waits for P_young; P_young dies if requesting P_old's resource.

keywords.explain: Every process eventually gets the resource it needs and makes progress; no process waits indefinitely.
keywords.example: Because a restarted process keeps its old (smaller) timestamp, it becomes progressively older and will eventually always be allowed to wait.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='520' height='220' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='220' height='70' rx='10' class='hi'/><text x='120' y='40' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>P_i holds R</text><text x='120' y='62' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>timestamp T(P_i)</text><rect x='290' y='10' width='220' height='70' rx='10' class='bg'/><text x='400' y='40' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>P_j requests R</text><text x='400' y='62' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>timestamp T(P_j)</text><text x='260' y='55' text-anchor='middle' class='big ink'>?</text><rect x='10' y='120' width='220' height='60' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='120' y='145' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>T(P_i) < T(P_j)</text><text x='120' y='168' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ok'>P_j WAITS (older wins)</text><rect x='290' y='120' width='220' height='60' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='400' y='145' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>T(P_i) >= T(P_j)</text><text x='400' y='168' text-anchor='middle' class='sub no'>P_j ABORTED & restarted</text><line x1='120' y1='80' x2='120' y2='120' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arr)'/><line x1='400' y1='80' x2='400' y2='120' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><defs><marker id='arr' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='4' refY='4' orient='auto'><path d='M0,0 L8,4 L0,8 Z' fill='#7F77DD'/></marker></defs></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Decision tree: P_i holds resource R. P_j requests R. If T(P_i) < T(P_j), P_j waits. Otherwise P_j is aborted and restarted with same timestamp.

given.aim: Determine if the Wait-Die style scheme is both deadlock-free and starvation-free.

terms.meaning: P_i is older than P_j (was created earlier, has a smaller timestamp).
terms.example: T(P_i)=5, T(P_j)=10 means P_i is older.
terms.connects: If the holding process is older, the requesting process waits — this is the Wait part.

terms.meaning: P_j is killed and restarted but keeps its original timestamp.
terms.example: P_j with timestamp 3 is killed but restarted with timestamp 3, not a new one.
terms.connects: Keeping the old timestamp means repeated aborts make the process progressively 'older', guaranteeing eventual progress.

terms.meaning: Ensures that a circular wait can never form, making deadlock structurally impossible.
terms.example: Circular wait requires P1 waits for P2 and P2 waits for P1 — this scheme prevents that cycle.
terms.connects: Since waits only occur from younger to older (in timestamp order), a cycle is impossible.

given.plan: Check deadlock freedom: waits only go from younger processes to older ones (by timestamp), forming a strict partial order — no cycle possible, so no deadlock. Check starvation freedom: each time P_j is killed it keeps its old timestamp, so over time P_j becomes the 'oldest' process alive, after which it always gets to wait (never killed again), so it eventually completes.

steps.title: Verify Deadlock Freedom
steps.formula_raw: P_j waits only if T(P_i) < T(P_j) (P_i is older)
steps.apply: A wait-for edge P_j -> P_i only exists when T(P_j) > T(P_i), i.e., P_j is younger than P_i. Since timestamps are unique integers, the wait-for graph has edges only from higher-timestamp (younger) processes to lower-timestamp (older) processes. This is a strict partial order — a directed acyclic structure — so no cycle (circular wait) can form. Therefore the scheme is DEADLOCK-FREE.
steps.note: This is essentially the Wait-Die algorithm. Younger processes die; older processes wait. No cycle possible in the wait-for graph.

steps.title: Verify Starvation Freedom
steps.apply: When P_j is killed and restarted, it keeps its original (smaller) timestamp. Each time processes complete and leave the system, the surviving processes have smaller relative timestamps. Eventually, P_j's timestamp will be the smallest among all requesting processes for the needed resource. At that point T(P_i) >= T(P_j) is no longer possible for any holding process P_i (since P_j has the smallest timestamp), so P_j will always get to wait and will never be killed again. Hence P_j eventually gets the resource — the scheme is STARVATION-FREE.
steps.note: The key insight is retaining the original timestamp on restart. Without this, younger restarts could be killed indefinitely, causing starvation.

solution.result: The scheme is both deadlock-free and starvation-free. Answer: D.

questions.formula_note: Wait-Die deadlock prevention algorithm: older process waits, younger process dies (aborted); retaining timestamp on restart ensures starvation freedom.

questions.question:
A certain moving arm disk-storage device has the following specifications:
- Number of tracks per surface: 404
- Track storage capacity: 130000 bytes
- Disk speed: 3600 rpm
- Average seek time: 30 ms

Estimate the average latency, the disk storage capacity, and the data transfer rate.
questions.answer: Average latency = 8.33 ms; Disk storage capacity = 404 * 130000 bytes = 52520000 bytes (approx 52.52 MB for a single surface); Data transfer rate = 7800000 bytes/sec (approx 7.8 MB/s)
questions.to_find: Calculate average rotational latency, total disk storage capacity, and data transfer rate.

understand.plain: A hard disk spins at 3600 rotations per minute. The average time to wait for the right sector to come under the head is half a rotation (average latency). Multiplying tracks by bytes per track gives storage capacity. The transfer rate is how fast bytes flow off the disk per second at that spin speed.

keywords.explain: On average, the desired sector is half a rotation away when the head arrives; so latency = time for half a revolution.
keywords.example: At 3600 rpm, one revolution = 60/3600 = 1/60 s = 16.67 ms; half = 8.33 ms.

keywords.explain: The rate at which data passes under the read/write head; equals track capacity divided by the time for one full revolution.
keywords.example: 130000 bytes per track / (1/60 s per revolution) = 7,800,000 bytes/s.

keywords.explain: Total bytes that can be stored; for a single surface it is (number of tracks) x (bytes per track).

understand.visual_alt: Disk spinning at 3600 rpm. One full revolution takes 16.67 ms. Average latency is half that = 8.33 ms. 404 tracks each holding 130000 bytes.

given.aim: Compute average latency, storage capacity, and data transfer rate from disk specs.

terms.meaning: The disk makes 3600 revolutions per minute.
terms.example: 60 revolutions per second, so one revolution = 1/60 s = 16.67 ms.
terms.connects: Used to compute rotational period and hence latency and transfer rate.

terms.meaning: Each track on a single surface holds 130000 bytes.
terms.example: A full revolution reads 130000 bytes off one track.
terms.connects: Dividing by revolution time gives transfer rate; multiplying by track count gives capacity.

terms.meaning: There are 404 concentric tracks on each recording surface.
terms.example: A disk with 2 surfaces would have 808 tracks total.
terms.connects: Total capacity = 404 x 130000 bytes (for one surface as given).

given.plan: Step 1: compute period of one revolution from rpm. Step 2: average latency = half revolution. Step 3: capacity = tracks x bytes/track. Step 4: transfer rate = bytes/track divided by revolution period.

steps.title: Compute Rotational Period
steps.note: This is the time for one complete revolution of the disk platter.

steps.title: Average Rotational Latency
steps.note: On average the desired sector is halfway around from where the head currently sits.

steps.title: Disk Storage Capacity
steps.apply: Capacity = 404 x 130000 = 52,520,000 bytes = approx 52.52 MB (per surface)
steps.note: This assumes a single recording surface. If multiple surfaces are present, multiply by the number of surfaces.

steps.title: Data Transfer Rate
steps.note: This is the sustained transfer rate once the head is over the correct track and sector.

solution.result: Average latency = 8.33 ms; Disk storage capacity = 52,520,000 bytes (~52.52 MB per surface); Data transfer rate = 7,800,000 bytes/sec (~7.8 MB/s).

questions.formula_note: Disk performance formulas: rotational period = 60/rpm; average latency = period/2; transfer rate = bytes_per_track x (rpm/60).

questions.question:
A certain moving arm disk storage, with one head, has the following specifications:
- Number of tracks per recording surface: (not given, single surface implied)
- Disk rotation speed: 2900 rpm
- Track storage capacity: 62500 bits

The average latency of this device is P ms and the data transfer rate is Q bits/sec. Write the values of P and Q.
questions.answer: P = 10.34 ms (average latency); Q = 3,020,833 bits/sec (approx 3.02 x 10^6 bits/sec)
questions.to_find: Find average rotational latency P (in ms) and data transfer rate Q (bits/sec) for a disk at 2900 rpm with 62500 bits per track.

understand.plain: The disk rotates at 2900 rpm. Average latency is the time for half a revolution. The data transfer rate is how many bits pass under the head per second, computed as bits per track times revolutions per second.

keywords.explain: Half the time of one full disk revolution; the expected wait for the target sector to rotate under the read/write head.
keywords.example: At 2900 rpm: one rev = 60/2900 s = 20.69 ms; half = 10.34 ms.

keywords.explain: The number of bits transferred per second while reading a track sequentially.

understand.visual_alt: Disk spinning at 2900 rpm. Each track stores 62500 bits. One revolution = 60/2900 = 20.69 ms. Average latency = half revolution = 10.34 ms. Transfer rate = 62500 x 2900/60 bits/s.

given.aim: Compute average latency P and data transfer rate Q from the given disk parameters.

terms.meaning: The disk makes 2900 revolutions every minute.
terms.connects: Determines both the revolution period (for latency) and the rate at which track data is read.

terms.meaning: Each track stores 62500 bits (= 7812.5 bytes) of data.
terms.example: Reading one complete revolution transfers 62500 bits.
terms.connects: Multiplied by revolutions/second gives the data transfer rate Q.

terms.meaning: Expected rotational delay = half a revolution time.
terms.example: If one revolution = 20.69 ms, then P = 10.34 ms.

given.plan: Compute one revolution time from rpm. Halve it for P. Multiply bits per track by revolutions per second for Q.

steps.title: Compute Period of One Revolution
steps.note: 2900 rpm means 2900 full disk rotations every 60 seconds.

steps.title: Compute Average Latency P
steps.note: On average, the desired sector is half a revolution away when the head arrives at the track.

steps.title: Compute Data Transfer Rate Q
steps.note: This equals approximately 3.02 x 10^6 bits/sec or about 3.02 Mbits/s.

solution.result: P = 10.34 ms (average latency); Q = 3,020,833 bits/sec (approx 3.02 Mbits/s).

questions.formula_note: Disk performance: average latency = (60/rpm)/2 seconds converted to ms; data transfer rate = bits_per_track x (rpm/60) bits per second.
